content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcar PRACTTcBs C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 t 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
December 6,2001
Salina Jessie-Edwards
P.O. Box 37
Orland, CA 95963
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-274
Dear Ms. Jessie-Edwards:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the disclosure provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTION
Is a promissory note that you inherited from your husband's estate reportable on
your statement of economic interests?
CONCLUSION
Payments that aggregate $500 or more in a calend ar year are reportable as
income. Additionally, if the loan was made to a business, you have an economic interest
in that business and would report it as an investment. See discussion below.
FACTS
Your late husband was the holder of an unsecured promissory note from
Mr. Nunzio Femino. The note was never repaid to your late husband and was bequeathed
to you under his will. The probate of his estate settled on October 3,200L
I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Tifle 2, sections
1 8 I 0 9 - 1 8 997, of the California Code of Regulations.
ffi


Advice Letter No. A-01-274
Page2
Mr. Femino is currently in the process of going out of business. Neither you nor
your late husband received payments from Mr. Femino on this note. You did not indicate
in your letter if the original loan to Mr. Femino was in his personal capacity, or if the loan
was made to his business.
ANALYSIS
As a member of the Orland City Council, you must disclose, among other things,
all investments in business entities and all sources of income located in or doing business
in the City of Orland. (Sections 87206 and87207.)
An "investment" is defined as "any financial interest in or security issued by a
business entity, inoluding but not limited to common stock, preferred stock, rights,
warrants, options, debt instruments and any partnership or other ownership interest
owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official...." (Section82034.)
"Income" is defined as "a payment received, including but not limited to any
salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, including any
gift of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by the
filer...." (Section 82030.)
Your spouse held a promissory note from Nunzino Femino that you inherited
through the probate of his estate. In your facts, you did not indicate if the loan was made
to Mr. Femino's business or to Mr. Femino in his individual capacity. If the promissory
note was made to his business, and the unpaid balance on that note is $2,000 or more, it
represents a debt instrument issued by a business entity and is an investment.
(Schimandle Advice Letter, No. I-92-463.)
If the promissory note was issued to Mr. Femino in his individual capacity, you
must disclose on your statement of economic interests any payments you receive that
aggregate $500 or more in a calendar year. You indicated that he has yet to make any
payments. If that is the case, you have not received income to report. However, the
Act's disclosure provisions do not necessarily mirror the Act's conflict of interest
provisions. Since you have a legally binding agreement for payments (the promissory
note), Mr. Femino is a source of income to you and there may be instances when you
may not participate in a decision directly affecting Mr. Femino.2
You further ask if you need to amend your statement of economic interests to
report this inheritance. As a public official, you disclose your economic interests on an
annual basis. The statements you file each April generally disclose investments and real
property held, and income received, during the prior calendar year. A public official is
2 "Income" for purposes of Section 87103(c), the conflict ofinterest provisions, includes any
source of income. . . aggregating five hundred dollars ($5001 or more in value provi ded or promised to . . 1he
public official. @mphasis added.) "For purposes of the Political Reform Ac! a public official's income
includes income which as been promised to the public official at the time he or she has a legally
enforceable right to the promised income." @egulation 18703.3(a).)


Advice Letter No. A-01-274
Page 3
not expected to amend the statement each time during the year when an investment or
real property interest is acquired or disposed. That transaction is reported on the annual
statement filed each April. In your instance, there may be an exception. If the
promissory note was a loan to Mr. Femino's business, you and your spouse have held an
economic interest in his business and this should have been reported as an investment on
your statement for each year that you or your spouse held the note. If this is the case, and
you need more assistance in preparing the amendment schedules, please call me for
further assistance.
I trust this answers your questions zufficiently. Please feel free to contact me if
you have any additional questions.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
rETljt

QUESTION: Is a promissory note that you inherited from your husband's estate reportable on
your statement of economic interests?

CONCLUSION: Payments that aggregate $500 or more in a calend ar year are reportable as
income. Additionally, if the loan was made to a business, you have an economic interest
in that business and would report it as an investment. See discussion below.
